column1: # THE SCRAMBLE FOR AFRICA — A Heart of Darkness Event Web
# HOW TO ADD AN EVENT: append a row. No "start" or "end" — events link to each other freely.
#   description  the prose. Use \n for paragraph breaks.
#   image        which scene plate to show. One of: brussels | coast | river | inner | title
#   pullquote    a quote from Conrad's Heart of Darkness (or leave blank).
#   choiceN      the button text for option N (2–4 supported).
#   effectsN     space-separated stat changes. Format: stat+N or stat-N.
#                Stats: territory  gold  military  humanity  infamy
#   nextN        what event comes next. Options:
#                  (blank) or *      → random event from the entire web
#                  id1|id2|id3       → random pick from this list
#                  some_id           → always go to that specific event
# Lines starting with # are ignored. Save and refresh.

column3: It is 1884. Around a table you see representatives from Great Britain, France, Germany, Portugal and Austria-Hungary
column5: There was a vast amount of red — good to see at any time\, because one knows that some real work is done in there.
column6: Claim the Congo basin for the Company.
column12: Sign the Acts but ask for nothing today. Strike from the shadows later.
column15: Argue for an "international" Free State that you secretly run.

column3: Your colonists find their way to "your" newfound land yet you are faced with an obstacle to your peaceful colonization.
column6: Force the natives off "your" land and leave them be.
column9: Propose peaceful co-exsistance with the natives
column12: Slaughter the natives and prove your dominance.
column15: Set up missions to attempt to convert them

column3: After liberating the land from these "savages" you are met with violence in the name of revenge.
column6: Renounce your land and apoligize to the natives
column9: Demand an aplogogy for their behavior as you graciously left them with their lives.
column12: Mark you territory with blood and make sure you will not be triffled with again.

column2: Landfall at the Mouth of the River
column3: The plane air drops you on a beach of black sand. Customs huts lean into the surf. A Belgian sergeant in a sweat-stained collar asks whether you want "porters or prisoners — they cost the same."
column5: I've seen the devil of violence\, and the devil of greed\, and the devil of hot desire.
column6: Hire free porters at fair wages.
column12: Refuse both and march inland with your own men.

column3: A boiler rusts in the long grass. An overturned railway truck lies on its back\, wheels in the air. Six men in iron collars file past you balancing baskets of earth. The overseer nods as if you were neighbors meeting on a Sunday boulevard.
column5: They were called criminals\, and the outraged law had come to them\, an insoluble mystery from the sea.
column6: Demand the chains be loosed for water.
column9: Walk on. Note it in your journal.
column12: "Where are the rifles\, the rivets\, the medicine?"

column3: You step aside from the path into a shadow under the trees its much colder under here. The shadow is full of shapes black, crouched, lying, sitting between the trees. Half coming out, half effaced within the dim light. A young man near you lifts his head. You have a Swedish ship's biscuit in your pocket.
column5: They were dying slowly — it was very clear. They were not enemies\, they were not criminals\, they were nothing earthly now.
column6: Place the biscuit in his hand.
column9: Walk on. There is nothing to be done for one of them.
column12: call him a filthy animal and spit at him

column3: The Central Station Manager has eyes the color of a winter fish. Mr. Kurtz‚ he says, almost in confidence, sends in more ivory than all of us combined. He is also‚ shall we say‚ unwell.
column5: He was commonplace in complexion\, in feature\, in manners\, and in voice. He inspires uneasiness.
column6: Volunteer to relieve Kurtz at the Inner Station.
column9: Quietly sabotage Kurtz's reputation in your dispatches.
column12: Side with the Manager publicly; visit Kurtz secretly.

column3: A tribal chief offers a treaty drawn up by your translator. The chief cannot read French. He is told it concerns "friendship and trade." It is\, in fact\, a deed transferring 40‚000 square miles to the Company in perpetuity.
column5: To tear treasure out of the bowels of the land was their desire\, with no more moral purpose at the back of it than there is in burglars breaking into a safe.
column6: Witness his X and pour the brandy.
column9: Translate the document honestly. Walk away if he refuses.
column12: Forge the X yourself once he sleeps.

column3: Three chiefs sit cross legged in a clearing, weighing whether to let your column pass north. Their warriors line the trees, spears bright in the dawn.
column5: The earth seemed unearthly. We are accustomed to look upon the shackled form of a conquered monster\, but there — there you could look at a thing monstrous and free.
column6: Offer guns and gin in exchange for safe passage.
column12: Show your Maxim gun. Fire once into the air.

column3: Father Renaud appears out of the bush in a torn cassock, leading a column of orphans. He has been told you are "a man of conscience."
column5: The conquest of the earth\, which mostly means the taking it away from those who have a different complexion\, is not a pretty thing when you look into it too much.
column6: Take the orphans into your service as porters and houseboys.
column9: Build him a chapel and a clinic at the station.
column12: Have him quietly escorted back to the coast. He sees too much.

column3: A white fog stands all around the steamer like something solid. From the bank rises a long\, modulated cry — and then nothing. The pilgrims cock their rifles.
column5: We were wanderers on a prehistoric earth\, on an earth that wore the aspect of an unknown planet.
column9: Steam on. Order them to fire at any sound.
column12: Steam on slowly. Hold their fire.

column3: Sticks — little sticks — whiz past your face. Your Kru helmsman leaves the wheel to fire through the shutter\, and a long cane spear comes through the opening and drops him at your feet. The shaft brushes your shoes.
column5: He had no restraint\, no restraint — just like Kurtz — a tree swayed by the wind.
column6: Bury him onshore with what dignity is possible.
column9: Tip the body into the brown current. The Manager would.
column12: Carry him to the Inner Station to be honored.

column3: Around every campfire someone speaks of Kurtz. He has built a kingdom of skulls. He has written a treatise. He has scrawled at the bottom of the last page\, in a different hand: "Exterminate all the brutes."
column5: The wilderness had patted him on the head\, and\, behold\, it was like a ball — an ivory ball.
column6: March on the Inner Station to confront him.
column9: Send word to Brussels denouncing him before he denounces you.
column12: Quietly take credit for his ivory shipments.

column3: Slender posts ring the long house — and through your glass the round ornaments on top resolve into shrunken heads\, all facing inward except one\, which faces out\, as if for greeting. A gaunt Russian in patched motley waves both arms: "You don't understand. He has enlarged my mind."
column6: Take the ivory. Burn the heads. Tell Brussels nothing.
column9: Wrestle Kurtz aboard and bring him back as evidence.
column12: Kneel and listen. Stay until the chanting ends.

column3: By dawn three of your men are dead and the cook is shaking like a leaf. The doctor's last quinine ran out a week ago.
column5: It was paddled by black fellows. You could see from afar the white of their eyeballs glistening.
column6: Push on at half-speed. Leave the sick on the bank.
column9: Halt the column for ten days until the fever passes.
column12: Trade your last rifles to a passing trader for medicine.

column3: An Arab trader in white robes offers you a deal: rifles for slaves‚ slaves for ivory‚ ivory for gold. The whole circle complete in a single afternoon.
column5: The word ivory rang in the air\, was whispered\, was sighed. You would think they were praying to it.
column6: Take the deal. Look the other way.
column9: Refuse and confiscate the caravan in the Company's name.

column3: A column of smoke rises from the stockade you built last month. Runners arrive panting: the porters have risen. Two overseers hang from the flagpole.
column6: March back and put it down with the Maxim gun.
column9: Negotiate. Pardon the leaders if they return to work.
column12: Burn the village to the foundations as an example.
column15: Abandon the post and press north. There is more land.

column3: A sealed dispatch arrives by runner. The Company is "concerned by recent reports" and "invites your immediate return for consultation."
column5: His was an impenetrable darkness. I looked at him as you peer down at a man who is lying at the bottom of a precipice where the sun never shines.
column6: Burn the letter. Pretend it never came.
column9: Comply. Sail home and face the directors.
column12: Reply with a ledger of ivory tonnage and a crate of tusks.
